findings.title: Malformed balanceOf can turn failed settlement accounting into a successful zero-output route
findings.why_it_matters: The router relies on balance deltas to prove every leg and final settlement. `_erc20BalanceOf` silently returns zero when `balanceOf` reverts or returns malformed data, so a route with zero minimums can emit success and skip settlement/refund for a token whose balance cannot be measured, leaving produced tokens stranded in the router instead of reverting atomically.
evidence: src/FameRouter.sol:305-308 treats failed or short `balanceOf` responses as balance 0.
src/FameRouter.sol:101-112 computes gross output and settlement from that route-local balance; if both mins are zero, zero output transfers are no-ops and the call can succeed.
findings.suggested_fix: Make route-asset balance reads fail closed: revert on failed or malformed ERC20 `balanceOf` for any token in the route snapshot, and add a malformed/reverting balanceOf token test that proves execution reverts without stranded route-local assets.

findings.title: Deployment validation does not prove enabled venues, so a broadcast router can pass config checks while all routes still revert
findings.why_it_matters: The deploy script broadcasts a router with no venue families or venue targets enabled, and the validation script only checks fee recipient, fee ppm, skip-NFT state, and manifest launchability. Once the fixture manifest is marked launchable, this validation path can still miss a deployed router that rejects every production route with `VenueFamilyDisabled` or `VenueTargetDisabled`.
evidence: script/DeployFameRouter.s.sol:14-16 deploys `FameRouter` and stops broadcasting without calling `setVenueFamilyEnabled` or `setVenueTargetEnabled`.
script/ValidateFameRouterBase.s.sol:24-28 performs no venue-family or venue-target assertions.
findings.suggested_fix: Either configure the expected Base venue families/targets during deployment or make validation require every launch fixture's family and target to be enabled before the router is considered safe to launch.

findings.title: Fork validation can silently skip or run unpinned while the launch gate only checks fixture counts
findings.why_it_matters: The fork test skips entirely when `BASE_RPC` is unset and falls back to latest Base head when `PINNED_BASE_BLOCK` is zero. `isLaunchable()` does not require a nonzero pinned block, so a future manifest count update can make the validation script pass without proving deterministic, pinned Base execution.
evidence: test/router/FameRouterForkBase.t.sol:15-18 uses an unpinned fork when `pinnedBaseBlock()` returns 0.
findings.suggested_fix: Require a nonzero pinned Base block in `isLaunchable()` and in the fork tests, and make CI/release validation fail rather than skip when `BASE_RPC` is missing for launch-blocking router checks.

residual_risks: I did not find a persistent `_executing` flag leak: ordinary reverts roll back the flag, and `rescue` is also protected by `nonReentrant` during settlement callbacks.

Approval cleanup after a target revert appears atomically rolled back by EVM transaction semantics, but it still needs adversarial token coverage to prove non-standard approve behavior is handled as intended.
testing_gaps: No malformed or reverting `balanceOf` token test proves route accounting fails closed.
No validation test asserts all production fixture venue families and targets are enabled on the deployed router.
Fork validation currently has no hard failure for missing `BASE_RPC` or zero pinned Base block.
